name: An Screig Rua
description: The Red Crag.
folklore: Features unique iron-rich staining in the limestone, giving it a distinct hue in the rain.
nature: Red-tinted limestone pavement with deep, dark grikes.

name: An Buaile Mór
description: The "Large Pasture" of Sruthán.
folklore: The primary grazing area for the village’s livestock, representing communal agricultural effort.
nature: A vast, walled enclosure with some natural soil accumulation.

name: An Suicín Thiar
description: The "Western Suicín."
folklore: A continuation of the marshy patch where water naturally collects from the Eochaill heights.
nature: A damp hollow filled with rushes and water-loving vegetation.

name: An Buaile Beag
description: The Small Pasture.
folklore: A miniature enclosure used for individual animals or "hospital" grazing.
nature: A tiny walled field, often the result of centuries of stone-clearing.

name: An Port Buí
description: The Yellow Port.
folklore: A small cove with yellow sand, a rarity on the northern "broken" limestone coast.
nature: A small, sandy inlet sheltered by low limestone cliffs.

name: The "Priest’s Wall"
description: A high wall in Cill Rónáin.
folklore: Historically associated with the Catholic clergy's efforts to define and protect their domain from the "Jumpers."
nature: A high, well-maintained stone wall that blocks the Atlantic wind.

name: An Carraig Dubh
description: The Black Rock.
folklore: An outcrop that remains wet and dark even in dry weather due to moss coverage.
nature: Covered in dark mosses and specialized lichens.

name: Gort an Dúidín
description: Field of the Pipe.
folklore: Named after a legendary smoker or a found clay pipe in the soil.
nature: A small, rectangular field with high dry-stone walls.

name: The "Eviction Stone"
description: A marker near the Land League meeting site.
folklore: Commemorates the resistance of the islanders against the Digby bailiffs and the "rent-war."
nature: A natural stone integrated into the historical narrative of Cill Rónáin.

name: An Log
description: The Hollow.
folklore: A natural amphitheater used for "whispering consultation" and secret meetings during the Land War.
nature: A deep, sheltered depression in the limestone.

name: An Gort Mór
description: The Large Field in Eoghanacht.
folklore: One of the few fields large enough for communal potato planting or "meitheal."
nature: Level limestone soil with a high concentration of clover.

name: An Pholl Dubh
description: The Black Hole.
folklore: A deep swallow-hole or cave entrance. Robinson notes these as sites where water "sucks" into the island's core.
nature: Surrounded by ferns and often containing standing water after gales.

name: An Carraigín
description: The Little Rock.
folklore: A navigational landmark used by currach fishermen to identify the northern approach.
nature: A distinct limestone outcrop near the shoreline.

name: Buaile na gCapall
description: Pasture of the Horses.
folklore: Historically used to graze the O'Flaherty clan's horses.
nature: A larger enclosed field with some natural grass cover.

name: The "Alder Tree" Site
description: Location near the Mainistir burial ground.
folklore: Site where the last saintly tree was cut down in 1947, an event Robinson links to the loss of "Aran simplicity."
nature: A small enclosure once capable of supporting a single tree.

name: An Carraig Mhór
description: The Big Rock.
folklore: A massive navigational hazard near the northern "broken cliffs."
nature: A massive limestone boulder partially submerged in the Atlantic.

name: Buaile na nEach
description: Pasture of the Steeds.
folklore: Associated with the high-status horses of the medieval O'Flaherty chieftains.
nature: A sheltered field with high, protective stone walls.

name: The "Steamer" Pier
description: The main landing point for the SS Duras.
folklore: Robinson describes its construction as the "transformation of the seaward aspect of Cill Rónáin."
nature: A modern (late 19th-century) stone pier made of massive limestone.

name: An Gort Glas
description: The Green Field.
folklore: A rare and precious fertile enclosure amidst the stony townland of Fearann an Choirce.
nature: Lush green grass protected by high stone walls.

name: An Chreig Mhór Refuge
description: A specific section of the crag used in 1920.
folklore: The location where a woman hid a money-box under her petticoats as Black and Tans burst into her house.
nature: Characterized by high stone walls and proximity to the village center.

name: The "Minister's House"
description: The old rectory in Cill Rónáin.
folklore: Associated with Rev. Kilbride, who was described by locals as a "bigot" and a "parson in council."
nature: A large stone building set in its own grounds, now largely disused.

name: Log na nGadhar
description: Hollow of the Dogs.
folklore: Local lore suggests it was a site where hunting dogs were traditionally kept or trained.
nature: A sheltered depression in the limestone.

name: An Suicín Theas
description: The "Southern Suicín."
folklore: A continuation of the poor quarter where men gathered to "exchange stories."
nature: Known as the "corner of lies" (Coirnéal na mBréag).

name: The "Post Office" Hill
description: A rise in Cill Rónáin village.
folklore: Location where islanders gathered to wait for news and mail from the SS Duras.
nature: A paved rise in the built-up area of the village.

name: An Carraigín Rua
description: The Little Red Rock.
folklore: Named for the red-tinted lichens that grow on its sheltered side.
nature: A small outcrop near the coast road.

name: Bóithrín na bPágánach
description: Lane of the Pagans.
folklore: Folklore suggests this was an ancient route used before the arrival of St. Enda.
nature: A very narrow and ancient-looking lane with high, mossy walls.

name: An Screig Bhán
description: The White Crag.
folklore: Named for its brilliant white appearance when the limestone is dry.
nature: Pure, unweathered limestone pavement with few grikes.

name: Buaile na gCluas
description: Pasture of the Ears.
folklore: A field with a cryptic name, possibly referring to a site of keen hearing or "listening" for the sea.
nature: A sheltered field near the northern coast.

name: An Carraig Dubh
description: The Black Rock (Coastal).
folklore: A navigational landmark for the northern fishing grounds.
nature: A dark, jagged rock visible at low tide.

name: Gort an tSionnaigh
description: Field of the Fox.
folklore: Robinson notes this as an ironic name since foxes are not native to the islands.
nature: A small, rocky field in the Eochaill townland.

name: An Port Beag
description: The Small Port.
folklore: A tiny inlet used for launching currachs in calm weather.
nature: A small, stony beach sheltered by limestone headlands.

name: The "CDB Cottages"
description: Semi-detached dwellings along Bóithrín na gCottageachaí.
folklore: Built in 1915 as part of the "Killing Home Rule by Kindness" government policy.
nature: Identical stone dwellings that represent the first "modern" housing on the island.

name: An Carraigín Bán
description: The Little White Rock.
folklore: A landmark used for aligning fishing nets in the northern sound.
nature: A small, bright limestone feature on the shoreline.

name: Gort na gCloch
description: Field of the Stones.
folklore: A field that remains incredibly stony despite generations of clearing effort.
nature: Covered in loose limestone fragments and "broken" rock.

name: An Buaile Dubh
description: The Black Pasture.
folklore: Named for the dark, peaty soil found in this specific limestone depression.
nature: A low-lying hollow with slightly darker topsoil than the surrounding crags.

name: Carcair na gCeann
description: Slope of the Heads.
folklore: Sometimes linked to ancient battle sites (1584) where skulls were allegedly unearthed.
nature: A steep rise with many loose limestone fragments.

name: The "Grotto" Madonna
description: A statue in the cliffs of An Aill Bhríste.
folklore: A blue and white figure that Robinson views as a "Crivelli" masterpiece in the wild.
nature: Nestled among "shadowy greenery" and flitting goldcrests.

name: An Screigín
description: The Little Crag.
folklore: Known for its absolute lack of soil; Robinson describes it as a "desert" of rock.
nature: Flat, weather-beaten limestone pavement.

name: Buaile na gCapall (Eoghanacht)
description: A horse pasture in the western townland.
folklore: Historically associated with the transport of seaweed to the internal fields.
nature: A larger enclosed field with some natural grass cover.

name: An Port Garbh (North)
description: A "Rough Port" on the northern Atlantic side.
folklore: Known for its "broken" cliffs and the extreme force of the Atlantic gales.
nature: A jagged coastline with massive limestone blocks.

name: An Chreig Mhór (Sruthán)
description: The "Great Crag" of Sruthán.
folklore: A massive expanse of limestone that served as a communal grazing area.
nature: A high, flat terrace of limestone with deep, protective fissures.

name: An Gort Glas (Mainistir)
description: The Green Field of Mainistir.
folklore: A rare and precious fertile field near the monastery ruins.
nature: Lush green grass protected by high stone walls.

name: An Suicín (Cill Rónáin)
description: The "marshy patch" south of the pub.
folklore: Robinson identifies it as the "poorest quarter" with terraces of thatched cottages.
nature: A damp, low-lying area where water collects.

name: An Buaile Bán
description: The White Pasture.
folklore: Named for the bleached appearance of the limestone fragments in the soil.
nature: A dry, stony field used for winter grazing.

name: The "Cairn" of the Dead
description: A small mound near Log na Marbh.
folklore: Believed to mark a specific grave from the 1584 O'Flaherty battle.
nature: A small heap of stones weathered by time.

name: An Pholl Dubh (Eochaill)
description: The Black Hole of Eochaill.
folklore: A deep swallow-hole in the limestone pavement.
nature: Surrounded by ferns and containing standing water.

name: An Gort Mór (Fearann an Choirce)
description: The Large Field of the Hill Farm.
folklore: One of the most fertile fields on the island, used for potato cultivation.
nature: Level limestone soil with some clover.

name: An Buaile Glas
description: The Green Pasture.
folklore: A particularly fertile enclosure in the townland of Corrúch.
nature: Features thick, nutritious grass for cattle.

name: Dún Chonchúir (Fort of Conor)
description: A massive, elliptical stone fort commanding the island’s highest pinnacle, characterized by monumental dry-stone terracing.
folklore: Associated with the Fir Bolg, the site served as the setting for a legendary banquet in 1857. Seventy members of the British Association, led by Sir William Wilde acting as cicerone, celebrated the island's antiquities here. Wilde addressed the party upon the structures, though islanders reportedly told "facetious stories" about the monuments' origins.
nature: The surrounding terrain is a "Vesuvian" landscape of massy, lava-colored limestone. The terraces provide a sense of "Alpine grandeur," with the grey rock appearing nearly molten in its geological intensity.

name: Dún Fearbhaigh
description: A prominent architectural cashel with formidable stone walls, strategically positioned to command the eastern approaches.
folklore: Regarded by antiquaries like Westropp as a primary "defence-line" for the island. Tradition holds that these walls were built to endure for millennia, only subsiding into the ocean after centuries of guarding against invaders from the mainland.
nature: Situated on a topographic scarp overlooking the Atlantic, the fort is constantly buffeted by the "boisterous ocean" and marine spray from the storm-beaten cliffs below.

name: Cill Cheannannach
description: A diminutive, early ecclesiastical ruin of monastic origin, noted for its primitive masonry and coastal proximity.
folklore: Dedicated to Saint Ceannannach. George Petrie identified this site as a monastic foundation, refuting earlier "Celtomaniac" theories that such structures were pagan fire-temples. It is celebrated as a landing place for saints who sought the "uncontaminated springs" of the islands.
nature: The site offers a "sea-parenthesis" view, where the horizon is framed by the grey limestone. It sits within a coastal environment where the land seems to merge with the Atlantic swell.

name: Tobar Cheannannach
description: A sacred holy well located near the shore, preserved as a significant site of traditional devotion.
folklore: A focal point for the "Aranite" pilgrimage, particularly during the patterns of the saints. John O’Donovan and George Petrie documented the site’s continued status as a place for spiritual solace and historical preservation amidst the rocky landscape.
nature: Often found nestled within the "chinks" of the limestone pavement, the well is a rare source of freshwater in a landscape defined by parched, barren rock.

name: Synge’s Cottage (Teach Synge)
description: A traditional thatched cottage that served as the residence and linguistic laboratory for playwright J.M. Synge.
folklore: Nicknamed "Ollscoil na Gaeilge" (The University of Irish), it became a shrine for the Gaelic League. Figures like Pádraic Pearse and Fr. Eugene O’Growney stayed here. Lady Gregory, however, expressed a passionate "galling jealousy" of Synge’s deep connection to the islanders, wishing to be "alone among the fishers."
nature: The cottage is integrated into the "labyrinth" of thousands of field-walls and limestone terraces that define the central townlands.

name: Cathaoir Synge (Synge’s Chair)
description: A natural stone seat perched precariously on the edge of the western cliffs.
folklore: This site represents Synge's "matchless matching of place and person." Here, he observed island girls in their "dull red" petticoats, which he felt harmonized perfectly with the grey limestone. Lady Gregory’s own visits to these heights were marked by her envy of Synge’s aesthetic intimacy with the island.
nature: Positioned above a vertical drop into the "boisterous ocean," the site is frequently obscured by heavy marine spray and the roar of the Atlantic.

name: Poll an tSéideáin (The Puffing Hole)
description: A dramatic natural blowhole formed by the relentless marine erosion of the limestone strata.
folklore: Visitors often viewed this as an "awfully sublime" feature of the Romantic landscape. It represents the "primitive" power of the island that so fascinated 19th-century scholars and "sentimental tourists."
nature: Erosion has created a vertical shaft; during heavy swells, the Atlantic is forced through the rock, resulting in a towering plume of salt spray and air.

name: Baile an Mhothair / Baile an Teampaill / Baile an Lorgain
description: The primary, densely clustered townlands situated upon the limestone pavement.
folklore: In 1893, these villages hosted the Haddon and Browne Anthropometric Laboratory. The researchers observed the "Aranite" face—long, oval, and well-marked—concluding the inhabitants were a "pure" race of primeval Gaelic stock.
nature: Newcomers often react with "consternation" at the "desert" appearance of these settlements, where houses are built directly onto the bare, lava-colored rock of the terraces.

name: Carraig an Phíobaire (The Piper’s Rock)
description: A distinct limestone feature steeped in musical and subterranean lore.
folklore: Legend tells of a piper who entered the cave of "An Uamhain" on the neighboring island of Inis Oírr. His music was reportedly heard traveling under the sound between the islands, eventually emerging from beneath this rock on Inis Meáin.
nature: A singular landmark within the "interminable and disheartening" labyrinth of stone field-walls.

name: Leaba Rónáin (Ronan's Bed)
description: A sacred grave site at the foot of a burial ground, traditionally used for curative rituals.
folklore: Islanders would sleep in the "leaba" on August 15th to seek cures. Folklore warns of a man who uprooted an elderberry bush here and was "smitten with a stroke." In 1947, Parish Priest Fr. Killeen controversially cleared the alder trees to allow for religious processions.
nature: Once characterized by a rare "forest" of alder trees in a landscape where such vegetation is virtually unknown. These trees grew wherever the saint was said to have walked.

name: An Aill Bhríste (The Broken Cliff)
description: A sharp vertical rock-face and carcair (slope) located near the old teachers' residence.
folklore: Features a "grotto" in a nook of the cliff containing a blue-and-white Madonna statue. Synge described the area as a "tapestry" of rambling roses and "old man’s beard," where the bright and the shadowy greenery met.
nature: A sanctuary for wildlife, including the goldcrest, whose bright crown appears as a "bright streak" amidst the "shadowy greenery" and rambling roses.
